title: Soft pronunciations: ť, ď, ň and ě
summary: Read the spelling patterns that signal soft consonants and j-like sounds.
objectives: Recognize ť, ď, and ň before ordinary vowels
Interpret d/t/n before i, í, and ě as soft
Recognize selected soft pronunciations by listening

sections.heading: Written ť, ď, ň
sections.body: Before a, o, u, or at the end of a word, the soft consonants are written with a háček.

sections.heading: Before i, í, and ě
sections.body: The háček is not written on d, t, or n before i, í, or ě; these spellings signal the soft pronunciations ď, ť, and ň.

sections.heading: p, b, v, m + ě
sections.body: Before ě, p, b, and v are pronounced with a following j sound. The sequence mě is pronounced approximately mně.

vocabulary.note: Final ň is written with háček.

resources.title: Original lesson: Soft Pronunciations
resources.note: Source for ť/ď/ň and ě spelling patterns.

exercises.prompt: How is the initial consonant in tělo pronounced?
exercises.explanation: Before ě, written t signals the soft consonant ť.

exercises.prompt: Type the Czech word for city.
exercises.explanation: The word is město; the sequence mě is pronounced approximately mně.

exercises.prompt: Which spelling is pronounced approximately Vjera?
exercises.explanation: v + ě produces a j-like glide.

exercises.prompt: Listen and choose the spelling you hear.
exercises.explanation: The word is tělo; t before ě is pronounced softly.

exercises.prompt: Listen and type the Czech word.
